title: OAuth 1.0a — Three-Legged Flow
description: The complete OAuth 1.0a authorization code flow. Consumer obtains a Request Token, redirects the user to authorize, receives a verifier, exchanges for an Access Token, and makes a signed API call. Each step shows the full HMAC-SHA1 signature construction.

steps.label: POST /oauth/initiate

request_headers.Authorization: OAuth realm="https://api.example.com", oauth_consumer_key="dpf43f3p2l4k3l03", oauth_nonce="wIjqoS", oauth_callback="http%3A%2F%2Fconsumer.example.com%2Fcb", oauth_signature_method="HMAC-SHA1", oauth_timestamp="137131200", oauth_version="1.0", oauth_signature="74KNZJeDHnMBp0EMJ9ZHe%2FLfEeA%3D"

steps.explanation: The Consumer begins the OAuth dance by requesting a temporary credential (Request Token) from the Service Provider. The request is signed with HMAC-SHA1 using only the Consumer Secret (token secret is empty at this stage). The oauth_callback tells the SP where to redirect the user after authorization.
annotations: No oauth_token is sent in this request — the consumer only has its consumer_key and consumer_secret at this point.
The signing key is consumer_secret + "&" + "" (empty token secret), resulting in a trailing ampersand.
The oauth_nonce must be unique per request to prevent replay attacks.
Response includes oauth_callback_confirmed=true per OAuth 1.0a spec (RFC 5849 §2.1).

steps.label: Redirect → /oauth/authorize
steps.explanation: The Consumer redirects the User's browser to the Service Provider's authorization endpoint, passing the Request Token obtained in Step 1. No signature is required for this redirect.
annotations: The consumer sends a 302 redirect to the user's browser pointing to the SP's /authorize endpoint.
The request token (oauth_token=hh5s93j4hdidpola) is passed as a query parameter.
The user must log in to the Service Provider if not already authenticated.

steps.label: GET /oauth/authorize
steps.response_body: Grant access to Example Consumer App?
steps.explanation: The user's browser follows the redirect and loads the Service Provider's authorization page. The SP uses the oauth_token to look up what the Consumer is requesting access to and presents the user with an Authorize/Deny prompt.
annotations: The SP validates the oauth_token against its database of issued request tokens.
The authorization page shows the Consumer's registered name and the permissions being requested.
The user must explicitly grant or deny access — OAuth 1.0a requires user consent.

steps.label: POST /oauth/authorize (Grant)
steps.explanation: The user clicks 'Authorize'. The Service Provider marks the Request Token as authorized, generates a one-time-use verifier code (oauth_verifier), and redirects the user's browser back to the Consumer's callback URL. This verifier prevents CSRF attacks during the token exchange.
annotations: The SP ties the oauth_verifier to the specific request token — it can only be used once.
The oauth_verifier is cryptographically random and short-lived.
If the user clicks 'Deny', the SP redirects with oauth_problem=user_refused instead.
RFC 5849 §2.2 — the verifier MUST be passed back via the callback URL.

steps.label: Callback with oauth_verifier
steps.explanation: The user's browser follows the SP's redirect to the Consumer's callback URL. The Consumer's server receives the oauth_token (to confirm which request token was authorized) and the oauth_verifier (to use in the next step when exchanging for an Access Token).
annotations: The Consumer must verify that the oauth_token matches the one it originally received in Step 1.
The oauth_verifier will be used in Step 6 to prove to the SP that the user granted consent.
The Consumer stores the verifier and proceeds to exchange the request token.

steps.label: POST /oauth/token

request_headers.Authorization: OAuth realm="https://api.example.com", oauth_consumer_key="dpf43f3p2l4k3l03", oauth_token="hh5s93j4hdidpola", oauth_nonce="walatlh", oauth_verifier="hfdp7dh39dks9884", oauth_signature_method="HMAC-SHA1", oauth_timestamp="137131201", oauth_version="1.0", oauth_signature="gKgrFCywp7rO0OXSjdot%2FIHRoVg%3D"

steps.label: 1. Collect OAuth Parameters
steps.value:
oauth_consumer_key=dpf43f3p2l4k3l03
oauth_nonce=walatlh
oauth_signature_method=HMAC-SHA1
oauth_timestamp=137131201
oauth_token=hh5s93j4hdidpola
oauth_verifier=hfdp7dh39dks9884
oauth_version=1.0

steps.label: 4. Build Signing Key (NOW includes Request Token Secret)
5. Compute HMAC-SHA1 and Base64 Encode
steps.value:
HMAC-SHA1(signing_key, base_string) → raw bytes → Base64
→ gKgrFCywp7rO0OXSjdot/IHRoVg=

steps.explanation: The Consumer exchanges the authorized Request Token for a long-lived Access Token. This request must be signed with the consumer_secret AND the request_token_secret (obtained in Step 1). The oauth_verifier proves the user granted consent. On success, the SP returns a new Access Token and Access Token Secret.
annotations: Key difference from Step 1: the signing key now includes the request token secret (hdhd0244k9j7ao03).
The oauth_verifier is consumed here — the SP marks it as used and it cannot be reused.
The request token (hh5s93j4hdidpola) is now invalidated by the SP.
The Access Token (nnch734d00sl2jdk) and its secret (pfkkdhi9sl3r4s00) should be stored securely by the Consumer.

steps.label: GET /api/photos (protected)

request_headers.Authorization: OAuth realm="https://api.example.com", oauth_consumer_key="dpf43f3p2l4k3l03", oauth_token="nnch734d00sl2jdk", oauth_nonce="kllo9940pd9333jh", oauth_signature_method="HMAC-SHA1", oauth_timestamp="1191242096", oauth_version="1.0", oauth_signature="tR3%2BTy81lMeYAr%2FFid0kMTYa%2FWM%3D"

response_body.note: Binary JPEG image data returned (42 KB)

steps.label: 1. Collect ALL Request Parameters (OAuth + Query String)
steps.value:
file=vacation.jpg
oauth_consumer_key=dpf43f3p2l4k3l03
oauth_nonce=kllo9940pd9333jh
oauth_signature_method=HMAC-SHA1
oauth_timestamp=1191242096
oauth_token=nnch734d00sl2jdk
oauth_version=1.0
size=original

steps.label: 5. Compute HMAC-SHA1 and Base64 Encode
steps.value:
HMAC-SHA1(signing_key, base_string) → raw bytes → Base64
→ tR3+Ty81lMeYAr/Fid0kMTYa/WM=

steps.explanation: The Consumer makes a signed API call to the protected resource using the Access Token. IMPORTANT: query string parameters (file, size) must be included in the signature base string along with OAuth parameters — failing to include them is a common implementation bug. The signing key uses the Access Token Secret obtained in Step 6.
annotations: The signing key now uses the access token secret (pfkkdhi9sl3r4s00) — different from the request token secret.
Query parameters (file=vacation.jpg, size=original) MUST be included in the signature base string.
This is a common source of bugs: developers forget to include query params in the OAuth signature.
The Access Token can be reused for subsequent API calls until it expires or is revoked.
